review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for reinforcement questions on this unit's evaluation concepts. The key distinctions, caveats, and examples are stated directly, so no separate clip is needed.

question_bank.question: What is the main lesson of the MMLU implementation example?
choices: Benchmark scores can change substantially because of seemingly minor prompting or scoring decisions
All MMLU implementations give identical rankings
Multiple-choice tasks are always robust to formatting
Constrained decoding is always the best method
question_bank.explanation: The point is that benchmark scores are not purely properties of the model. Formatting, prompting, and scoring conventions can materially change results.

question_bank.question: Why might relabeling answer options or changing formatting alter rankings on a multiple-choice benchmark?
choices: Because multiple-choice tasks are actually open-ended
Because generation and answer selection are sensitive to the exact surface form of the choices
Because accuracy cannot be computed after relabeling
Because only humans can score such tasks
question_bank.explanation: Even for simple-looking multiple-choice tasks, models can respond differently depending on how choices are formatted or constrained, which changes final scores.

question_bank.question: What kind of evidence would most strongly raise suspicion of benchmark contamination from pretraining?
choices: A model performs similarly on old and newly created items
A model does far better on older public problems than on newer analogous ones
A model uses chain-of-thought prompting
A model has low perplexity on validation data
question_bank.explanation: If a model performs unusually well on older public items but drops on newer comparable ones, that pattern suggests it may have seen benchmark content or close variants during training.

question_bank.question: Which mitigation is most directly aimed at keeping public benchmark tuning from exhausting the test set?
choices: Use only BLEU and ROUGE
Use private, regenerated, or dynamic test sets
Average more public leaderboards together
Remove all multiple-choice tasks
question_bank.explanation: Private, refreshed, or dynamic evaluation sets make it harder for systems to overfit a fixed public test set through repeated tuning or leakage.

question_bank.question: Which heuristic is mentioned as a possible leakage check?
choices: Whether the model answer is very short
Whether the model is unusually overconfident or drops when example order is scrambled
Whether the model uses beam search
Whether the benchmark includes images
question_bank.explanation: Leakage can sometimes show up as suspiciously confident answers or brittleness when the original presentation order is disrupted.
